Injection sites and techniques
Parenteral routes of drug administration are highly valuable 
and often underused in guinea pigs. The use of a local 
anaesthetic cream designed for application to the skin, 
such as combination lidocaine 2.5% and prilocaine 2.5% 
(EMLA cream), can help to reduce the discomfort and 
stress of injections (Wagner et al., 2006; van Oostrom and 
Knowles, 2018). Care should be taken not to overdose 
guinea pigs with local anaesthetic agents, although studies 
have shown that there is no significant absorption of these 
agents through the skin (Wagner et al., 2006). A small hair 
clip can be useful to mark the area of application (Figure 
4.1) or the hair can be clipped at the site. The manufacturer 
recommends applying the local anaesthetic cream 1 hour 
before the procedure and covering the site with an occlu-
sive dressing. However, the authors have observed a 
clinical benefit after as little as 5 minutes and do not rou-
tinely cover the site.
A butterfly catheter or winged infusion set is helpful for 
4.2
giving subcutaneous fluids.
Intramuscular injections may be used with care; the 
clinician should be mindful that the muscle bellies in 
guinea pigs are small. Subcutaneous injections should be 
considered, where appropriate, for larger volumes or in 
particularly fractious patients as they appear to yield 
comparable clinical results for many medications. As with 
subcutaneous injections, insulin syringes and needles are 
sufficient for most individuals, with some requiring a larger 
bore needle. The epaxial (lumbar) or quadriceps (cranial 
thigh; Figure 4.3) muscles are recommended sites for 
intramuscular injection (Autefage et al., 1990). The caudal 
thigh should be avoided due to the risk of iatrogenic dam-
age to the sciatic nerve (Baxter and Evans, 1973). Injec-
tions are best given with an assistant holding the guinea 
pig and being sure to support the chest and rump. The 
muscle should be gently massaged after the injection to 
help distribute the medication and reduce discomfort.

Chapter 4 • Basic techniques
Intramuscular injection techniques
To inject into the epaxial/lumbar muscle: 
■  Palpate the dorsal vertebral spinous process in 
the lumbar area where the muscle mass is 
largest
■  Palpate the corresponding lateral vertebral 
spinous process. The epaxial muscle belly lies 
between these processes 
■  Enter the muscle belly with the needle perpen-
dicular to the skin 
■  Take care to avoid placing the needle at full 
length or too laterally, which could cause it to  (a)

using a 1 ml syringe, or by inserting sterile stainless steel 
suture. If the blockage cannot be cleared, the needle 
should be removed and replaced with a new needle using 
the same insertion point. Improper placement of the needle 
or penetration of both cortices can cause extravasation 
of medications or fluids and associated pain.

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Positive training techniques for oral 
medications
Even the most well handled guinea pigs are sometimes 
averse to being handled for the administration of oral 
medications, and aversions to novel foods may also 
develop early in life (Tynes, 2010). Positive reinforcement 
techniques for oral dosing can assist owners and veteri-
nary surgeons to achieve stress-free medication. This 
training should be undertaken from an early age, ideally 
before the animal requires any medications.
When using the positive reinforcement method, a 
desired behaviour is rewarded with a positive experience. 
(a)
Depending on the individual guinea pig, this reward could 
be a favourite food, chin scratches or gentle petting. The 
desired behaviour – in this case, accepting oral medication 
delivered by a syringe – should be gradually achieved in 
small steps. The initial step is to introduce the presence of 
a syringe; when the animal initiates the desired behaviour 
(interaction with the syringe), the consequence is a positive 
response (a high-value reward) (Figure 4.6). Gradual train-
ing, from interaction with the syringe to ultimately taking 
medicine from it, will require time and practice.
Desensitization can be critical for medicating guinea 
pigs as their flight (or freeze) response will often be initiated 
by changes in the environment that are undetectable by 
(b)
humans (Brown, 2012). Desensitization can begin in the 
home by placing syringes in the animal’s enclosure for short 
periods of time and under observation. Guinea pigs can be 
curious and may interact directly with the syringes by chew-
ing on them when they are in their normal environment.
Counterconditioning is another technique used to 
diminish fear responses. Counterconditioning links a posi-
tive experience to a negative or aversive experience, with 
the aim of the positive experience outweighing the nega-
tive one. This can be facilitated by using a syringe contain-
ing a tasty food so that the unpleasant nature of the syringe  (c)
being presented is outweighed by the value of the food  Positive reinforcement techniques incorporating

that scent marking is a natural behaviour. Choice and

separation or neutering of non-compatible or competing 
boars should be considered as part of scent gland man-
agement. Diseases should be excluded through diagnos-
tic testing (see Chapter 5) before frequent scent gland 
cleaning is recommended.

a humane death. The ideal euthanasia process should

the body may vary between veterinary clinics, but they 
often include:
■  The body returning home with the owner for burial

teaching and learning purposes.
Memorials or small keepsakes such as footprints or fur 
samples are often greatly appreciated by clients. To collect 
footprints, the foot can be pressed into an appropriately 
coloured ink pad and then gently transferred on to a piece 
of card (Figure 4.10). Fur samples can easily be collected 
using scissors or clippers. It is ideal to try to get a repre- References and further reading
